summary: After writing a blog post about misunderstanding stock options, Julia Evans started asking questions about equity compensation in a public Slack channel at work. The discussion became so popular that a quarter of the company joined a dedicated #equity channel within days. She discovered that open discussion of confusing compensation topics benefits everyone because it reveals that nobody fully understands these issues. The post advocates for transparency around equity compensation in the workplace.
key_points: Public discussion of equity compensation at work benefits everyone and reveals shared confusion
A quarter of the company joined the #equity Slack channel immediately, showing massive pent-up demand for this information
Fear of being perceived badly for asking about stock options (especially questions implying you might quit) turned out to be unfounded
Even experts like lawyers and finance people were willing to engage openly with employee questions
money_quotes: It turns out that talking about confusing equity / compensation issues in public channels at work is awesome.
Everyone realizes that nobody else fully understands what's going on.
People with more experience can share what they know, and people with less experience can ask all their questions!
A quarter of the company joined the channel immediately.

I was originally worried that asking questions that amount to 'what can I do with my stock options if I quit?' would somehow be a bad idea or perceived badly. But mostly everyone seems really delighted to talk about it.
key_insight: Openly discussing confusing compensation topics at work helps everyone because it surfaces shared ignorance and creates space for collective learning.
